summary: Stancil argues that we conflate “databases” with structured, analyzable data, while the far larger opportunity is an “informationbase” that indexes and retrieves useful facts, documents, and context without heavy modeling. Snowflake’s Document AI hints at a shift from data warehousing toward Google‑like information retrieval, even if the most likely explanation is simply customer demand. The broader point is that search and retrieval for operational questions may matter more to most organizations than analytics-grade data pipelines.
money_quotes: A database isn’t just a collection of information; it’s a tightly organized catalog of capital-D Data.
Google’s “database” is none of these things.

We usually want information more than we want data, and the appeal of a chatbot to tell us the answer we want, without having to do the complicated work of retrieving and interpreting the data ourselves.
Any technology that makes solving those problems a prerequisite to using it is a technology that’ll be out of reach for most people.
So the real conclusion from Summit may be that: Though they’re doing it more slowly than Microsoft, Snowflake is gradually building a bomb of their own.
key_insight: The most valuable next step for data platforms may be indexing and retrieving information, not just storing analyzable data.
